_meta.name: English
_meta.notes: Phase 1a — first wave of UI chrome. Adds Settings panel, panel tabs, generation action buttons, status bar, and ~15 most-common toast messages. Per the bucketing decision, AI generation parameters (Steps, Sampler, CFG Scale, etc.), sampler/scheduler names, and feature names like ADetailer, ControlNet, LoRA stay English in every locale and are not instrumented at all.

settings.canvas.title: Canvas
settings.generation.title: Generation
settings.generation.highPrecision.toolti: Saves high-precision float data alongside each image. Eliminates banding and gives the Develop module ~0.5 stops of highlight headroom. Adds ~0.5–3s and ~12 MB per image. Best with fp32 VAE.
settings.outputFormat.title: Output Format
settings.vram.gpuWeights.description: How much VRAM to use for model weights. 0 = Forge default. Higher values keep more of the model in VRAM for faster generation; lower values offload weights to RAM to free VRAM for inference. Peak usage during generation may still exceed this — it's a loading budget, not a hard cap.
settings.extensions.title: Extensions
settings.extensions.description: Toggle which extensions appear in the Extensions tab.
settings.theme.title: Theme
settings.language.title: Language
settings.language.label: Interface language
settings.language.note: AI generation parameters (Steps, Sampler, CFG Scale, etc.) stay in English to match the terminology in tutorials and guides.
settings.accessibility.title: Accessibility
settings.updates.title: Updates
settings.defaults.title: Defaults
settings.defaults.description: Save current workflow settings as defaults for future sessions.

toast.vaeSwitchPending: VAE will switch after generation completes

develop.hpBadge.tooltip: High Precision: float32 source pixels active
develop.hpBadge.composited: High Precision: float buffer composited with AD/brush canvas pixels
develop.pick.suffix: Click to pick, then click the image. Shift-click to clear.
develop.pick.wb.tooltip: White balance — pick a neutral pixel that should render as gray
develop.pick.whitePoint.tooltip: White point — pick the brightest pixel that should render as pure white
develop.pick.blackPoint.tooltip: Black point — pick the darkest pixel that should render as pure black
develop.presets.title: Presets
tooltip.bigOutputWarning: Output will exceed 2000px on one or both sides. This may cause significant VRAM usage and slow generation on most hardware.
tooltip.histogramAutoStretch: Stretch input black/white to histogram 0.1% / 99.9% percentile
panels.upscale.hint: Single-call pipeline. Refine and ADetailer are independent: tick either, both, or neither. Steps/Denoise apply to the refine pass.
lora.sort.name: Name
lora.openFolder.tooltip: Open LoRA folder in file manager
lora.hint: Click to insert · Right-click for options
lora.empty.none: No LoRAs found in your models/Lora directory
codex.popover.home.title: Quick Reference
codex.popover.home.text: Search above or browse by category.
gallery.dup.title: Find duplicates
gallery.dup.hint: Stricter = only near-identical. Looser = minor edits count as duplicates. Max 25% difference.
gallery.dup.libraryMissing: imagehash library not installed. Restart Studio to install, or run pip install imagehash in your venv.
gallery.dup.readyTotal: All {total} images hashed. Ready to scan.
gallery.dup.needHashing: {hashed} / {total} images hashed. {missing} need hashing before scanning.
gallery.dup.noDuplicates: No duplicates found at this threshold.
workshop.title: Workshop
workshop.refresh.tooltip: Rescan every model, LoRA and VAE directory
workshop.vae.hint: Baked into the chain's final output. Override per-row from a row's ⚙ menu if you need a different VAE on an intermediate result.
workshop.keepIntermediates.tooltip: Keep each row's output as its own .safetensors file (useful for multi-row boards)
workshop.testMergeBtn.tooltip: Hot-swap UNet weights — no disk write, instant iteration
workshop.inspector.archMismatch: ⚠ Architecture mismatch — merge is not possible
workshop.health.knownArtifact: Known artifact — unused CLIP encoder layers. Not a merge issue.
workshop.health.allClean: No issues detected — all tensors look clean.
workshop.health.healthyClipArtifacts: Model is healthy. NaN keys are expected CLIP artifacts.
workshop.toast.nothingToDo: Nothing to do — fill at least one row, or add a LoRA / VAE
workshop.toast.testMergeApplied: Test merge applied in {time}s — generate to preview!
workshop.toast.testMergeWarnings: Test merge applied but validation had warnings
workshop.inspector.title: Inspector
workshop.method.weighted_sum.desc: The simplest merge — blends every weight between A and B by the alpha value. Good starting point for any merge. If results look washed out, try SLERP instead.
workshop.method.slerp.desc: Spherical interpolation — preserves the magnitude of weight vectors instead of averaging them. Usually produces sharper, more vibrant results than Weighted Sum at the same alpha. Best for blending two models of similar quality.
workshop.method.add_difference.desc: Extracts what Model B learned from Model C (its base), then applies that training to Model A. Use this to transplant a finetune's skills (e.g. anime style, specific subject) into a different model. Requires Model C — the base model B was finetuned from.
workshop.method.ties.desc: Trim, Elect Sign & Merge — extracts B's training over A, then trims away the weakest changes, keeping only the most significant ones. The Density parameter controls how aggressively to trim. Great for noisy finetunes where you want only the strongest signal.
workshop.method.dare.desc: Drop And REscale — randomly drops most of B's training changes and rescales the survivors to compensate. Neural networks are redundant, so even dropping 90% of changes often preserves the effect. Produces surprisingly clean results from messy finetunes.
workshop.method.dare_ties.desc: Combines DARE and TIES — randomly drops changes first, then trims the survivors by magnitude. The most aggressive filtering. Best when B is a very noisy finetune and you want only the absolute strongest signal.
workshop.method.cosine_adaptive.desc: Automatically computes a unique blend ratio for every weight based on how similar A and B are at that point. Where they already agree, it keeps A. Where they diverge, it incorporates B. The Shift parameter adjusts how conservative (positive) or aggressive (negative) the blending is. No alpha needed — the math decides.
workshop.method.star.desc: Spectral Truncation and Rescale — decomposes B's training via SVD and strips noisy components before merging. Produces cleaner results than element-wise methods, especially from overtrained or messy finetunes. The Eta parameter controls how aggressively noise is removed.
workshop.method.svd_struct_a_mag_b.desc: Decomposes both models via SVD, then takes A's feature directions (what the layer detects) and B's magnitudes (how strongly it responds). Example: photorealism model's composition + anime model's vibrancy. Produces results impossible from any weight-averaging method.
workshop.method.svd_struct_b_mag_a.desc: The inverse — takes B's feature directions (what the layer detects) and A's magnitudes (how strongly it responds). Same concept as Structure A + Mag B but swapped. Try both and compare — the results are surprisingly different.
workshop.method.svd_blend.desc: Aligns both models' spectral decompositions via Procrustes rotation, then interpolates structure and magnitude together in spectral space. Smoother than Weighted Sum because it respects the geometric relationship between feature directions rather than averaging raw weights.
workshop.method.della.desc: Like DARE but smarter about what it drops — drop probability is inversely proportional to magnitude, so large important changes survive while small noisy ones are more likely to be removed. Produces slightly more reliable results than DARE's uniform random masking.
workshop.method.della_ties.desc: Combines DELLA and TIES — magnitude-weighted dropout first, then trims survivors by magnitude. Like DARE-TIES but with smarter dropout that preferentially keeps important parameters.
workshop.method.breadcrumbs.desc: Dual-threshold trimming — like TIES but also removes the largest outlier changes, not just the smallest. Density controls the lower cutoff (how much to keep), Drop Rate controls the upper cutoff (how many outliers to remove). Best for finetunes with both noise and overfitting artifacts.
transformTool.rotArb.tooltip: Rotate layer by an arbitrary angle

education.pathPicker.title: How would you like to get started?
education.pathPicker.beginner.title: I'm new to AI image generation
education.pathPicker.beginner.desc: Walk me through making my first image and show me the tools.
education.pathPicker.experienced.title: I've used Stable Diffusion, Photoshop, or similar tools
education.pathPicker.experienced.desc: Show me where things are and what's different here.
education.pathPicker.skip: I'll figure it out on my own
education.cancelConfirm.walkthrough: End the walkthrough? You can restart it anytime from Settings.
education.settings.title: Education
education.tour.modelBranch.needModel.tex: Before we start, you need an AI model — the brain that generates images. You don’t have one installed yet. Here’s a good starter model to get going: Download ToxicHost’s recommended model Once downloaded, put the file in your Forge folder: models/Stable-diffusion/ Don’t close Studio — just drop the file in that folder.
education.tour.modelBranch.needModel.btn: I’ve put it in the folder
education.tour.modelBranch.refreshModels: Now click the refresh button (↻) next to the Model dropdown. Studio will scan for new models without needing a restart. Once your model appears in the dropdown, select it and we’ll get started.
education.tour.core.welcome.text: Welcome to Forge Studio. Let’s make your first image. See the text box on the right? Describe something you’d like to see — anything at all.
education.tour.core.generating.text: Working on it — watch the progress bar.
education.tour.core.firstImage.text: That’s yours. You just made that. No one else has this exact image. You can put it on your canvas to paint over it — try clicking Result → Canvas. Or hit Next to see what else is here.
education.tour.core.revealSettings.text: This panel is where you shape your results. A few things worth knowing right now: • Model — the AI brain. Different models, completely different styles. • Denoise — how much the AI changes existing canvas content. Your most important dial. Everything else — CFG, Steps, Seed, Sampler — you’ll learn by experimenting. The Codex tab explains each one when you’re ready.
education.tour.core.revealWorkspace.text: Toolstrip (left) — 19 painting tools. Start with B for brush and E for eraser. Layers (right panel) — generated images land on the bottom layer, your painting goes above. Regenerating never destroys your painted edits. This is a real canvas. Paint, generate, paint more, generate again.
education.tour.core.complete.text: That’s the core: describe, generate, paint, iterate. The rest of the UI is now open. Check the Codex tab anytime — it has reference docs for every tool, setting, and feature. Enjoy making things.
education.tour.inpaint.paint.text: Your image is on the canvas now — ready to work with. Pick up the brush (B) and paint something on top of it. Scribble, add a shape, change a color. Don’t worry about being precise.
education.tour.inpaint.regenerate.text: Now hit Generate again. The AI sees your paint strokes and incorporates them. It reinterprets the whole canvas with your edits as input — not just pasting paint on top. The Denoise slider controls how much it changes. Lower = keep more of what’s there.
education.tour.inpaint.masking.text: What if you only want to change part of the image? Press Q to enter mask mode. Your brush now paints a red overlay. The red tells the AI: "only change this area." Paint the mask → write what should go there → Generate. Only the masked area changes. Press Q again to exit. You’ll use this constantly.
education.tour.experienced.layout.text: Quick orientation. Toolstrip on the left (19 painting tools). Canvas in the center — scroll to zoom, Space+drag to pan. Panel on the right with Generate, Extensions, and Settings tabs. \ collapses it. Context bar above the canvas shows controls for the active tool.
education.tour.experienced.canvasRouting: No separate txt2img/img2img tabs. Studio checks the canvas and routes automatically: • Blank canvas → txt2img • Content on canvas → img2img • Mask painted → inpainting The pixels are the source of truth. Paint something, erase it all, generate — that’s txt2img because the canvas is blank. No mode toggles.
education.tour.experienced.canvasUx.text: A few things that work differently here: • Context bar values are scrub labels — drag horizontally to adjust, click to type a number. • Each tool remembers its own settings independently. Switch tools and back — settings preserved. • Shift+Drag on canvas adjusts brush size (horizontal) and opacity (vertical). • Ctrl+Click eyedrops from any tool without switching. • At 200%+ zoom, pixels render nearest-neighbor (crisp, not blurry).
education.tour.experienced.maskRegions.t: Mask mode (Q) uses the same brush engine — pressure, hardness, all of it. No separate mask canvas. Regional prompting is painted directly on the canvas. Add a region, paint its area with the region color, write a prompt. Uses pre-softmax attention bias, not output blending — small regions actually get their own content.
education.tour.experienced.layers.text: Real layer stack with blend modes, opacity, and reordering. Sending results to canvas creates a new layer — never overwrites your work. Generation results go to a reusable "Gen Result" layer. Your painted edits on other layers are always safe. Export the full stack as PSD.
education.tour.experienced.extras.text: ControlNet (2 units) can source from the canvas composite, active layer, or an upload. ADetailer (3 slots) runs automatically after generation. Drag and drop an image onto the canvas to load it — resizes canvas, imports metadata, creates a new layer. Save current settings as workflow defaults — persists server-side across sessions.
education.tour.experienced.shortcuts.tex: Key shortcuts to know: Q Mask mode • B Brush • E Eraser • V Transform [/] Brush size • Shift+Drag Size + opacity Ctrl+Enter Generate • \ Toggle panel Ctrl+Z Undo • Space+Drag Pan • Scroll Zoom Full list in the Codex tab (highlighted above) under Keyboard Shortcuts. You’re set. Go make something.
moduleTour.workshop.merge.text: This is the Workshop — where you merge and combine AI models. The merge panel has your Method, Weight slider, and model selectors. Pick two models, set a blend ratio, and merge.
moduleTour.workshop.actions.text: Test Merge hot-swaps the weights in memory — no file written. Generate some test images to see if you like the blend. Happy with it? Save to Disk writes the merged model as a new checkpoint.
moduleTour.workshop.history.text: The History tab keeps a journal of every merge you’ve done — recipe, settings, and notes. You can rate, tag, and search your experiments. Check the Codex for details on each merge method.
moduleTour.gallery.layout.text: This is the Gallery — a browser for all your generated images. Sidebar has folders and character tags for filtering. Search at the top matches filenames and metadata.
moduleTour.gallery.interact.text:
Click any image for a detail view with full metadata. Right-click for a context menu (rename, move, delete, send to canvas).

Drag images between folders in the sidebar to organize.
moduleTour.gallery.live.text: The gallery updates in real-time — new images appear automatically as they’re generated. The status dot in the topbar shows watcher status. Send to Canvas loads any image into Studio for editing.
showme.inpaint.intro.text: Here’s a simple scene. Let’s change part of it using inpainting. See the sun in the sky? We’re going to replace it with something else.
showme.inpaint.mask.text: Press Q to enter mask mode. Your brush now paints a red overlay instead of color. Paint red over the sun. It doesn’t need to be precise — just cover it.
showme.inpaint.prompt.text: A prompt has been pre-filled — "a full moon in a night sky" — but you can change it to whatever you want. The AI will only change the red area.
showme.inpaint.generate.text: Hit Generate. Only the masked area changes — the rest stays untouched. Afterward, press Q to exit mask mode. Try different denoise values to see how much changes.
showme.softInpaint.intro.text: Standard inpainting has a hard boundary — inside the mask changes, outside doesn’t. Soft Inpainting adds smooth blending at the boundary for more natural transitions. Let’s try it. First, press Q and mask an area — paint over the house.
showme.softInpaint.enable.text: Find Soft Inpainting in the Generate panel (it appears when mask mode is active). Enable the checkbox. You’ll see six parameters. Here’s what matters most: • Preservation — how much the original is kept outside the mask. Higher = less change. • Transition Contrast — how sharp the blend edge is. Lower = softer blend.
showme.softInpaint.generate.text: Hit Generate and compare the result to standard inpainting (disable Soft Inpainting and regenerate with the same mask to compare). Soft Inpainting works best with feathered masks — use a soft brush (low hardness) to paint your mask for the best blending. The other parameters (Schedule Bias, Mask Influence, Difference Threshold/Contrast) are fine at defaults until you need specific control.
showme.routing.blank.text: This is a blank canvas. If you hit Generate right now, Studio runs txt2img — generating from scratch based only on your prompt. No mode selection needed. The canvas state IS the mode.
showme.routing.content.text: Now paint something on the canvas — anything. A scribble, a shape, a splash of color. Once there are pixels on the canvas, Generate switches to img2img automatically. The AI works with what’s there. Denoise controls how much it changes.
showme.routing.mask.text: Now press Q to enter mask mode. Paint a red overlay over part of your content. With a mask present, Generate switches to inpainting — only the masked area changes. Everything else stays untouched. Three modes, zero toggles. The pixels decide.
showme.regions.intro.text: Regional prompting lets you assign different prompts to different areas of your canvas. Instead of one description for the whole image, you can say "forest on the left, city on the right."
showme.regions.add.text: Scroll down in the Generate panel to the Regions section. Click the + button to add a region. A new region appears with a colored swatch and a prompt field.
showme.regions.paint.text: Now paint on the canvas with the brush (B). The region’s color marks where this prompt applies. Paint the left half of the canvas, then type a prompt like "dense forest, green trees" in the region’s prompt field.
showme.regions.second.text: Add a second region with the + button. Paint the right half with the new color. Give it a different prompt like "modern city skyline." The main prompt (top box) has been pre-filled with a style — it sets the overall look for all regions.
showme.regions.generate.text: Hit Generate. Each region gets its own content based on its prompt. Small regions work too — Studio’s system is designed for this. Remember to clear regions when done — they persist until removed.
showme.controlnet.intro.text: ControlNet uses the structure of an existing image to guide generation. Let’s use this scene as our structural source. The AI will follow the layout (where the sky is, where the ground is, where objects are) but create new content.
showme.controlnet.enable.text: In the Extensions tab, find ControlNet Unit 1. Enable the checkbox. Set the Preprocessor and Model to a matching pair — for example, Canny preprocessor with a Canny model. Set Source to Canvas. Preprocessor and model must match in type AND architecture (SD1.5 vs SDXL).
showme.controlnet.prompt.text: A prompt has been pre-filled for you, but you can change it to whatever you like. Describe what the image should become while keeping this layout. The structural map preserves the spatial arrangement — where foreground and background are, where objects sit.
showme.controlnet.generate.text: Hit Generate. The result follows the spatial structure of the original but with completely new content. Weight controls how strongly the structure guides (1.0 = full). Start/End controls which denoising steps it applies to — try 0.0–0.5 for looser structure.
showme.layers.intro.text: This canvas has two layers. The Background layer has a blue square. Layer 1 has a red square overlapping it. Layers are transparent sheets stacked on each other.
showme.layers.visibility.text: Try clicking the eye icon on Layer 1 to hide it. The red square disappears, revealing the blue one underneath. Click it again to show it. This is how you control what’s visible without deleting anything.
showme.layers.generation.text: When you Generate, the result lands on the reference (bottom) layer. Your painting on upper layers is never overwritten. This means you can always regenerate without losing your edits.
showme.layers.blend.text: Each layer has Opacity and Blend Mode. Try changing Layer 1’s blend mode to Multiply or Screen to see how it interacts with the layer below. Export the full stack as PSD via the Export button.
showme.clone.intro.text: See the pink square on the ground? We’ll remove it using the Clone Stamp. This tool copies pixels from one area and paints them over another — perfect for removing unwanted objects.
showme.clone.select.text: Press K to select the Clone Stamp tool.
showme.clone.source.text: Now hold Alt and click on a clean area of green ground near the pink square. This sets your source point — where the clone copies from.
showme.clone.paint.text: Now paint directly over the pink square. The tool copies green ground from the source point, covering the pink. If the clone starts picking up wrong content, Alt+Click a new source and keep going.
showme.transform.intro.text: Here’s a shape on the canvas. Press V to activate the Transform tool. Drag the handles to resize, drag inside to move, and rotate from the corners.
showme.transform.basic.text: Try the basics: drag a corner handle to resize. Hold Shift to constrain proportions. Drag inside to reposition. Enter applies the transform. Escape cancels.
showme.transform.warp.text: In the context bar, you’ll see warp mode buttons: • Affine — free deformation with control points • Similitude — scale + rotate only (no skew) • Rigid — minimal distortion, natural repositioning • Perspective — four-corner pin Try each one to see how they differ.
showme.cnCanny.intro.text: Canny extracts edge lines from your image. The AI follows those edges while generating new content — maintaining architectural layouts, linework, and structural outlines.
showme.cnCanny.setup.text: In the Extensions tab: 1. Enable ControlNet Unit 1 2. Set Preprocessor to Canny 3. Set Model to a Canny ControlNet model 4. Set Source to Canvas Preprocessor and model must both be Canny, and match your checkpoint architecture (SD1.5 or SDXL).
showme.cnCanny.generate.text: A prompt has been pre-filled. Hit Generate — the result follows the edges of the original scene (house outline, tree shape, horizon line) but with completely new colors, textures, and content. Lower the Weight for looser edge following. Try Start/End 0.0–0.5 for structural guidance that fades during detail refinement.
showme.cnOpenpose.intro.text: OpenPose detects body poses — the position of head, arms, legs, and joints. The AI generates a new character following that exact pose. Here’s a simple figure. OpenPose will extract its pose.
showme.cnOpenpose.setup.text: In the Extensions tab: 1. Enable ControlNet Unit 1 2. Set Preprocessor to OpenPose 3. Set Model to an OpenPose ControlNet model 4. Set Source to Canvas The preprocessor detects the pose skeleton. The model uses it to guide generation.
showme.cnOpenpose.generate.text: Hit Generate. The result shows a new character in the same pose as the figure. OpenPose is great for: maintaining specific poses, transferring poses from reference photos, generating consistent character positions across images. You can also use a real photo as the source — set Source to Upload instead of Canvas.
showme.cnDepth.intro.text: Depth extracts a depth map — what’s close vs far. The AI preserves the spatial arrangement (foreground objects stay in front, background stays behind) while creating entirely new content.
showme.cnDepth.setup.text: In the Extensions tab: 1. Enable ControlNet Unit 1 2. Set Preprocessor to Depth (or Depth Anything) 3. Set Model to a Depth ControlNet model 4. Set Source to Canvas Note: Some Depth preprocessors require compatible GPU drivers. If you get an error, try Canny instead.
showme.cnDepth.generate.text: Hit Generate. The house, tree, and ground stay in their spatial positions but become entirely new content. Depth is ideal for: scene reimagination, style transfer while keeping composition, maintaining spatial relationships in complex scenes.
showme.radialSym.intro.text: Radial symmetry mirrors your brush strokes around a center point. Let’s paint a mandala pattern. First, select the Brush tool (B).
showme.radialSym.enable.text: In the context bar (top of the canvas area), find the symmetry buttons (near the right side). Click the radial button (the starburst icon). Set the axis count — try 6 for a snowflake pattern or 8 for a mandala. More axes = more repetitions.
showme.radialSym.paint.text:
Now paint on the canvas. Every stroke is replicated around the center point.

Try painting outward from the center, or in arcs. Even simple strokes create complex patterns.

The smudge tool also works with radial symmetry — each segment has its own independent smudge buffer.
showme.lora.intro.text: The LoRA Browser lets you visually browse and insert LoRAs without typing the syntax manually. Look for the LORA button near the prompt box in the Generate panel — click it to open the browser.
showme.lora.browse.text: The browser shows cards for each LoRA file in your models/Lora/ folder. Cards display the name and trigger words (read from sidecar .json metadata files). Use the search bar to filter by name or trigger word.
showme.lora.insert.text: Click a LoRA card to insert it into your prompt. The browser auto-inserts the <lora:name:weight> tag AND any trigger words at the preferred weight. You can stack multiple LoRAs — just click more cards. Lower individual weights (0.5–0.7) when stacking to prevent conflicts.
showme.dynamics.intro.text: The brush engine has a dynamics panel that controls advanced stroke behavior. Select the Brush (B) and look for the gear icon in the brush presets area below the context bar.
showme.dynamics.panel.text: The Brush Dynamics panel exposes: • Spacing — gap between brush stamps (lower = smoother, higher = dotted) • Size Jitter — random size variation per stamp • Opacity Jitter — random opacity variation • Scatter — random offset from stroke path • Rotation Jitter — random angle per stamp • Follow Stroke — stamps rotate to follow stroke direction
showme.dynamics.try.text: Try painting with different dynamics settings. High scatter + size jitter creates foliage and particle effects. High spacing creates dotted/stippled lines. The Scatter preset is pre-configured with dynamics for textured spray effects. Each preset stores its own dynamics.
showme.wildcards.intro.text: Wildcards insert random text into your prompts for automatic variety. The syntax is __name__ (double underscores). Each generation picks a different line from the matching text file.
showme.wildcards.browse.text: Click the WILDCARD button near the prompt box to open the wildcard browser. This shows all your installed wildcard files. Wildcard files live in extensions/sd-dynamic-prompts/wildcards/. Each .txt file is one wildcard — the filename becomes the name.
showme.wildcards.syntax.text: Try typing in your prompt: a __colors__ cat sitting on a __furniture__ Each generation replaces __colors__ with a random line from colors.txt and __furniture__ from furniture.txt. Folders work too: a file at wildcards/animals/cats.txt is used as __animals/cats__.
showme.wildcards.lexicon.text: The Wildcards tab (in the main tab bar) provides a full wildcard editor — folder tree, file creation, inline editing, and preview. Wildcards work in the negative prompt too. Common pattern: put your standard negatives in neg4.txt and use __neg4__ instead of typing them every time.

codex.entry.core_workflow.content:
Forge Studio\u2019s workflow is a loop: describe \u2192 generate \u2192 paint \u2192 iterate.

Type what you want to see in the prompt box, hit Generate, and the AI creates an image on your canvas. From there, you can paint on top of it, then generate again \u2014 the AI works with whatever is already on the canvas.

You\u2019re building images with your hands and the AI together. Every generation can be a starting point, a refinement, or a complete reimagining.
codex.entry.core_workflow.deep: Studio auto-routes based on canvas state: blank canvas \u2192 txt2img, canvas with content \u2192 img2img, mask painted \u2192 inpainting. The function isCanvasBlank() is the sole source of truth for this routing \u2014 it checks actual pixel data, not a dirty flag. Denoise strength controls how much the AI changes existing content (1.0 = full replace, 0.3 = gentle refinement).
codex.entry.tutorials_and_tours.content:
Studio has interactive tutorials that walk you through features hands-on, in the actual UI, on a real canvas. Three places to find them.

1. Show Me buttons in Codex. Many entries in this Codex have a blue Show Me button at the top. Click it and Studio opens a new tutorial document, sets the scene up for you, and walks you through the feature step-by-step. Currently available for: Inpainting, Regional Prompting, ControlNet, Layers, LoRA Browser, Brush Dynamics, Wildcards, Symmetry Painting, and the Core Workflow.

2. The first-run path picker. The first time you launched Studio, you saw a card with two options:

\u2022 I\u2019m new to AI image generation \u2014 progressive walkthrough that makes your first image, then reveals controls in groups as you go. Branches to a model-acquisition flow if Studio detects you don\u2019t have any models installed yet, and to an inpaint flow if you click Result \u2192 Canvas. Persists \u2014 if you close mid-walkthrough, it resumes where you left off.

\u2022 I\u2019ve used Stable Diffusion, Photoshop, or similar tools \u2014 spatial orientation tour. Full UI visible from the start, focuses on what\u2019s different about Studio compared to other AI/art tools.

You can also skip and figure things out yourself.

3. Restart from Settings. Open the Settings tab in the right panel and find the Education section. Two buttons:

\u2022 Restart Tour \u2014 replays your current path from the beginning.

\u2022 Change Path \u2014 brings the first-run picker back so you can switch between Beginner and Experienced.

None of these block you. You can dismiss any tour with Escape, and Show Me tutorials happen on a separate document so they never disturb your real work.
codex.entry.first_image.content:
1. Type a description in the prompt box \u2014 \"a cat on a mountain at sunset\" works fine.

2. Click Generate (or Ctrl+Enter).

3. Wait a few seconds. Your image appears in the output gallery.

4. Click Result \u2192 Canvas to place it on the canvas for editing.

Every image is unique \u2014 same prompt, different seed, different result. Try generating a few times to see the variety.
codex.entry.ui_layout.content:
Left: Toolstrip \u2014 your painting tools (brush, eraser, fill, and 16 more).

Center: Canvas \u2014 scroll to zoom, Space+drag to pan. This is where your image lives.

Right: Panel \u2014 three tabs: Generate (prompt, settings, output), Extensions (ControlNet, ADetailer), and Settings. \\ collapses it for more canvas space.

Top: Context bar \u2014 shows controls for whichever tool is active (size, opacity, hardness, etc.).

Bottom: Status bar \u2014 generation progress, VRAM usage, canvas dimensions.

Tab bar: Switch between Studio (canvas), Gallery, Video Lab, Workshop, Wildcards, and Codex (you\u2019re here).
codex.entry.models_and_vae.content:
The model (also called a checkpoint) is the AI brain that generates your images. Without one, Studio can\u2019t generate anything. Different models produce completely different styles \u2014 photorealistic, anime, painterly, abstract.

Getting your first model:

1. Download a model file. Here\u2019s a good one to start with: ToxicHost\u2019s recommended starter model

2. Put the downloaded file in your Forge folder: models/Stable-diffusion/

3. In Studio, click the refresh button (\u21bb) next to the Model dropdown. Your model appears \u2014 no restart needed.

4. Select it from the dropdown. Make sure the architecture selector (radio buttons: SD 1.5 / XL / Flux) matches your model \u2014 wrong architecture = errors.

Find more models on CivitAI (checkpoints, LoRAs, community content) and Hugging Face (official releases).

Architectures:

\u2022 SD 1.5 \u2014 smaller, faster, huge ecosystem. ~4 GB VRAM.

\u2022 SDXL / Illustrious \u2014 higher quality, more VRAM (6\u20137 GB). Illustrious models excel at anime and illustration styles.

\u2022 Flux \u2014 newer architecture with different prompt handling. Needs separate text encoders.

SD1.5, SDXL, and Flux checkpoints, LoRAs, and ControlNet models are not cross-compatible.

Model formats:

\u2022 .safetensors \u2014 standard, always prefer this.

\u2022 .gguf \u2014 quantized, uses less VRAM at some quality cost. Good for larger models on smaller GPUs.

\u2022 fp16 / fp8 \u2014 numerical precision. Lower = less VRAM, slightly less quality. fp16 is the sweet spot for most. Set \"Diffusion in Low Bits\" to \"Automatic\" for fp8/GGUF models.

VAE: The VAE handles color processing. Leave it on \"Automatic\" \u2014 most models include their own. If your colors look washed out or oversaturated, try switching to a specific VAE from the dropdown.
codex.entry.models_and_vae.deep: Model dropdown pulls from Forge\u2019s checkpoint directory. Hot-reload via refresh button \u2014 no restart. VAE \"Automatic\" uses baked-in or Forge global. For SD1.5 color issues try vae-ft-mse-840000. SDXL uses dual text encoders (CLIP-L + CLIP-G). Flux may require separate text encoder downloads. Some models need keywords in their path to be recognized (e.g. Flux-Kontext needs \"kontext\" in the filename).
codex.entry.output_workflow.content:
Generated images appear in the output gallery strip at the bottom of the Generate panel.

Result \u2192 Canvas places the image on your canvas as a new layer \u2014 it doesn\u2019t overwrite your existing work. Pipeline results (generation, upscale) go to a reusable \"Gen Result\" layer.

Save writes to disk (auto-save is on by default). You can change the format in Settings: PNG, JPEG, or WebP. Embed metadata stores your prompt and settings inside saved files so other tools (PNG Info, other AI UIs) can read them later.

Gallery always remembers: Studio\u2019s Gallery tracks generation params for every image regardless of the Embed metadata setting. It uses a pixel fingerprint, so metadata survives file renames, moves between folders, and format conversions (PNG \u2192 JPG \u2192 WebP). Turning off Embed metadata only affects whether other tools can read it from the file itself.

Filename convention: Auto-saved files use Studio-NNNNN-seed.ext, where NNNNN is a 5-digit counter per output folder and seed is the exact seed used for that image. Batched images get sequential counters and sequential seeds.

Workflow defaults: Save your current parameter settings (sampler, scheduler, CFG, steps, denoise, and more) as defaults via Settings \u2192 Defaults \u2192 Save Current as Defaults. These persist server-side and load automatically on next launch. Separate from per-tool brush settings, which are client-side.
codex.entry.output_workflow.deep:
Gallery metadata is keyed by SHA256 of the decoded RGB pixel data, computed locally in compute_content_hash(). Generation writes the metadata row to the Gallery DB immediately via save_metadata_by_hash(), without waiting for a Gallery scan. When the scan later finds the file on disk, it links the existing hash-keyed metadata row to the filepath-keyed image row. Because the key is the pixel fingerprint, not the path, operations that preserve pixel identity (rename, move, lossless format conversion) keep the metadata attached. Lossy re-encoding will break the link \u2014 the new file has different pixels, so it gets a new hash.

Filename counter is determined per-folder at save time by scanning for existing Studio-NNNNN-* files and incrementing the highest match, so manual deletions in a folder don\u2019t create gaps that get filled later.
codex.entry.color_picker.content:
Click the toolstrip swatches to open the color picker. HSV wheel + hex input for precise colors.

X swaps foreground and background colors. D resets them to black and white.

Ctrl+Click on the canvas samples a color from any tool without switching away. I switches to the dedicated eyedropper tool.

Studio keeps a rolling history of your last 10 colors as clickable swatches below the picker.
codex.entry.shortcuts_tools.content:
B Brush • E Eraser • I Eyedropper • G Fill/Gradient • U Shape • T Text

S Smudge • R Blur • J Dodge/Burn • K Clone • Y Liquify • P Pixelate

M Rect select • O Ellipse select • L Lasso • W Wand • C Crop • V Transform

Q Toggle mask mode • D Reset colors • X Swap FG/BG
codex.entry.shortcuts_canvas.content:
Ctrl+Z Undo • Ctrl+Shift+Z Redo

Ctrl+A Select all • Ctrl+D Deselect • Ctrl+Shift+I Invert selection

Ctrl+C/X/V Copy/Cut/Paste

Del / Backspace — context-sensitive:

• Active transform → cancel transform, discard its contents.

• Active selection → clear pixels inside the selection.

• No selection, no transform → resets the canvas — removes all layers and creates a fresh white Background. Undoable with Ctrl+Z.

Ctrl+Enter Generate • \ Toggle panel

Ctrl+] / Ctrl+[ Cycle documents

F or 0 Zoom to fit • Scroll Zoom • Space+Drag Pan

[/] Brush size • {/} Brush hardness • Shift+Drag Size + opacity adjust

Ctrl+Click Eyedropper from any tool • Alt+Click Clone source

Ctrl+Shift+N New layer • Ctrl+J Duplicate layer • Ctrl+E Merge down • Ctrl+Shift+E Flatten

Enter Commit transform • Escape Cancel / deselect
codex.entry.layers_basics.content:
Layers are transparent sheets stacked on each other. + adds a new layer, ▲▼ reorder, ⇩ merge down, eye toggles visibility. Each layer has its own Opacity and Blend Mode.

Generated images land on the reference layer (bottom). Your painting goes on paint layers above. This means regenerating never destroys your painted edits — they live on a different layer.

Blend modes (Normal, Multiply, Screen, Overlay, Soft Light, Hard Light, Difference, etc.) control how layers interact. Merge Down composites using the current blend mode and opacity.
codex.entry.psd_export.content:
Export your full layer stack as a PSD file that opens in Photoshop, Krita, GIMP, or any PSD-compatible editor. Click Export in the Layers section.

Layer names, blend modes, opacity, and visibility are all preserved. You can continue working in your preferred editor without losing your stack.
codex.entry.psd_export.deep: Built via ag-psd.js. Served via dedicated FastAPI route. Firefox uses POST + download (no showSaveFilePicker support).
codex.entry.inpaint_basics.content:
Inpainting lets you selectively change parts of an image while keeping the rest untouched. It’s one of the most powerful and commonly used features in Studio.

Step by step:

1. Have an image on your canvas (generate one or load one).

2. Press Q or click Mask. Your brush now paints a red overlay instead of color.

3. Paint red over the area you want to change.

4. Write a prompt describing what should appear in the masked area.

5. Hit Generate. Only the red area changes.

6. Press Q again to exit mask mode.

Whole Picture vs Only Masked:

Whole Picture generates the full canvas at your set resolution. The AI sees everything and the result blends naturally. Use for: removing objects, changing backgrounds, anything where context matters.

Only Masked crops just the masked region, scales it up, generates, then composites back. Much more detail for small areas. Use for: fixing faces, refining details. Increase the Padding slider so the AI sees context around the mask.

Fill mode — what goes under the mask:

• Original — keeps existing pixels as the starting point. Best for most tasks.

• Fill — replaces with solid average color. Best for removing objects — the AI fills naturally instead of building on top.

• Latent Noise / Latent Nothing — more creative freedom, less predictable.

Denoise guide:

• 0.3–0.5 — subtle refinement, artifact cleanup.

• 0.5–0.7 — moderate reshaping.

• 0.7–0.9 — major replacement.

• 1.0 — complete replacement, nothing preserved.

Blur: Softens the mask edge. Higher values (8–16) = smoother transitions. Use 0 for hard cuts.

Common recipes:

• Fix a face: Only Masked + Original + Padding 64–128 + Denoise 0.4–0.6.

• Remove an object: Whole Picture + Fill + Denoise 0.7–0.9. Describe replacement.

• Add something new: Original + Denoise 0.7–0.9. Describe the addition.

• Outpaint: Resize canvas larger, mask empty area, Fill + Denoise 0.8–1.0.
codex.entry.inpaint_basics.deep:
Mask is a separate canvas buffer, not a layer. "Only Masked" with padding = A1111’s "Inpaint at full resolution" — crops masked region plus padding, scales to gen resolution, generates, composites back with mask feathering.

Soft Inpainting (toggle visible when mask mode active): modifies the pipeline for smooth boundary blending. Parameters: Schedule Bias, Preservation, Transition Contrast, Mask Influence, Difference Threshold, Difference Contrast. Best with feathered masks. Adds processing overhead.
codex.entry.regional_prompting.content:
Regional prompting lets you assign different descriptions to different areas of your canvas. Instead of one prompt for the whole image, you can say "forest on the left, city on the right."

Step by step:

1. In the Generate tab, scroll to Regions and click + to add a region.

2. A new region appears with a color swatch and prompt field.

3. Switch to the brush (B) and paint on the canvas. The region’s color marks where it applies.

4. Type a prompt for this region — describe what should appear in the painted area.

5. Add more regions as needed. Each gets its own color and prompt.

6. Your main prompt still affects the whole image. Regions add local control on top.

7. Hit Generate.

Tips:

• Regions can overlap. Where they do, both prompts influence.

• Small regions work. Studio’s system is specifically designed for this — small painted regions get their own content regardless of how much canvas area larger regions occupy.

• Leave unpainted gaps between regions. Those gaps act as coherence bridges — lighting, perspective, and style flow through the background. Painting regions edge-to-edge with no gap tends to produce harder seams.

• Use the main prompt for overall qualities (lighting, style) and regions for specific content. The main prompt is automatically prepended to each region.

• Don’t forget to clear regions when done — they persist until removed.

For the technical architecture, see the Attention Couple entry below.
codex.entry.attention_couple.content:
Attention Couple is the name of the system that makes regional prompting work in Studio. You don’t interact with it directly — you paint regions and it handles the rest. This entry is for the curious.

What it does: When you paint multiple regions with different prompts, Attention Couple steers the model’s internal attention so each region’s content ends up where you painted it, without bleeding into the other regions.

Why it’s different from other regional systems: Approaches like Forge Couple, Regional Prompter, and ComfyUI node-based solutions generate separate outputs and blend them together, or bias the attention outputs after the fact. Studio biases the attention itself, before the softmax that decides what each pixel pays attention to. That means small painted regions actually get their own content instead of being washed out by larger neighbors.

Why unpainted gaps matter: Studio also applies regional isolation on self-attention (pixel-to-pixel feature sharing). Without it, features leak laterally between subjects and you get heterochromia, hair color bleed, and other attribute mixing. Unpainted areas act as a shared background that every region can attend to, which is what lets perspective and lighting flow through the whole image coherently.
codex.entry.attention_couple.deep:
v7 architecture — dual-control pre-softmax biasing:

• attn2_patch + attn2_replace: cross-attention (text → spatial) pre-softmax logit bias. All region conditionings concatenated into one K tensor; Q @ Kᵀ computed manually with a spatial bias matrix added before softmax. Bias scale tied to region area via 1/sqrt(frac), capped at 12.

• attn1_replace: self-attention (pixel → pixel) regional isolation bias. Same-region positions attend freely; cross-region attention is negatively biased (−8.0); background ↔ anything attends freely. This is the coherence bridge.

• Uncond pass strips region tokens — if uncond attends to region content without bias, CFG cancels regional separation.

• 1−t² decay curve holds spatial control through the detail refinement phase.

• Global prompt is prepended to each region encoding. Gaussian feathering + bilinear downsampling on the region masks.

Inspired by Bounded Attention (Dahary et al., ECCV 2024) which proved cross-attention bias alone cannot prevent attribute leakage. First implementation of combined cross+self attention masking for regional prompting in the Forge/A1111 ecosystem. AD integration: each region carries ad_prompt (character-only, post-wildcard-resolution) so ADetailer inpaints single faces with clean per-character descriptions rather than the scene-level global prompt.
codex.entry.controlnet.content:
ControlNet gives the AI structural guidance — "follow this pose," "use this edge map," "keep this depth layout." It analyzes a source image and uses that structure to guide generation.

Common use cases:

Keep a pose: Preprocessor → OpenPose, Model → OpenPose ControlNet. The AI follows the detected pose but creates new content.

Keep edges/lines: Preprocessor → Canny, Model → Canny ControlNet. Maintains architectural layouts, mechanical designs, linework.

Keep depth layout: Preprocessor → Depth, Model → Depth ControlNet. Foreground/background relationships stay the same.

Key settings:

• Weight (0.0–2.0): How strongly the structure guide affects the result. 1.0 = full.

• Source: Canvas (composite), Active Layer (just selected layer), or Upload (external image).

• Start/End: Which portion of denoising ControlNet is active. 0.0–0.5 gives structural guidance early without constraining fine details later.

Studio has 2 ControlNet units — combine them (e.g., pose + depth).

Important: Preprocessor and model must match in type AND architecture (SD1.5 vs SDXL).
